slide.title: The Execution Path
bullets: One query flows top-down: Driver → Catalyst → Tungsten → DAG → executors
Geometry is the lesson: stacked = sequential, side-by-side = parallel
Each stage hands off to the next; tasks within a stage run in parallel

narration.text: Every Spark query takes the same journey, from the driver down to the executors that do the work.

slide.title: The Driver
bullets: Runs your application code
Creates the SparkSession and the logical plan
Coordinates the whole job

slide.title: Catalyst Optimizer
bullets: Unresolved → Logical → Optimized → Physical plan
Rule-based and cost-based optimization
Produces the most efficient physical plan to run

slide.title: DAG Scheduler
bullets: Splits the job into stages at shuffle boundaries
Stages run in sequence; each waits for the previous shuffle
Each stage is a set of tasks — one per partition

slide.title: Cluster Manager
bullets: Allocates executors on the cluster
Pluggable backend: Local, Standalone, YARN, Kubernetes, Databricks
Independent of the rest of the execution path

slide.title: Executors
bullets: Run the dispatched tasks in parallel
Hold data partitions in memory
Report results back to the driver

slide.title: RDD API
slide.body:
## What's covered

- What an RDD actually is — the three letters of the name and the four properties
- Partitions: the unit of parallelism
- Lineage: how Spark recovers from failure without replicating data
- `SparkContext` — the low-level entry point for RDD work
- Creating RDDs: `parallelize`, `range`, `textFile`, from a DataFrame
- Transformations vs actions, RDD edition
- RDDs of objects — why we use bare tuples here, what "no schema" actually means
- Narrow vs wide transformations — the single most important performance distinction
- Pair RDDs — the `(key, value)` view that mirrors SQL `GROUP BY`
- `reduceByKey` vs `groupByKey` — the exam classic
- Persistence: `cache()`, `persist()`, storage levels
- Partition-level operations — `mapPartitions`, `repartition`, `coalesce`
- Shared variables — broadcast and accumulators
- RDD vs DataFrame — when to actually reach for each

narration.text: Before DataFrames existed, there were RDDs. Everything in Spark still runs on top of them, so understanding the RDD model explains how the whole engine behaves.

slide.title: What an RDD Actually Is
slide.body:
An **RDD** — Resilient Distributed Dataset — is the original Spark data abstraction. Every DataFrame you write today eventually compiles down to RDD operations under the hood, so understanding RDDs explains *why* Spark behaves the way it does: lazy evaluation, fault tolerance through lineage, and partition-level parallelism.

A useful mental shortcut: an RDD is a **lazy pure function applied over an immutable distributed dataset, with no metadata about its elements**. Resilience comes from the pure function plus lineage, not from any replication of the dataset itself.

Read the three letters of the name:

- **Resilient** — fault-tolerant. Lost partitions are recomputed from the recipe (lineage), not retrieved from a replica.
- **Distributed** — partitioned across executor memory on multiple machines.
- **Dataset** — a collection of records. A record can be any object — a string, a tuple, a custom class. Unlike a DataFrame, there is no schema and no column names.

Four more properties worth memorizing:

- **Immutable** — you never mutate an RDD. Every transformation produces a new RDD.
- **Lazy** — transformations are recorded; nothing actually runs until an action.
- **Schema-free** — there are no columns, just elements. You handle structure yourself in code.
- **Typed** — in Scala fully type-parameterized (`RDD[Int]`); in Python untyped at the language level but still typed at runtime.

narration.text: An RDD is a resilient distributed dataset. Resilient because Spark can rebuild any lost piece from its lineage. Distributed because it is split into partitions spread across the cluster. And immutable, so every transformation produces a brand new RDD.

slide.title: Partitions — the Unit of Parallelism
slide.body:
An RDD is split into **partitions** — chunks of data that can be processed independently. Each partition is processed by exactly one task, on one executor core. So:

- Number of partitions = degree of parallelism for that RDD.
- More partitions than cores → some tasks queue, but the cluster stays busy.
- Fewer partitions than cores → cores sit idle.

After a shuffle, the number of output partitions defaults to `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` (200 unless you change it). For RDDs created with `parallelize`, the default count is `sc.defaultParallelism`, which is usually the total number of cores available.

```text
            txn_rdd   —   12 records, numSlices=4
   +-----------+-----------+-----------+-----------+
   | Partition0| Partition1| Partition2| Partition3|
   |  T1 T2 T3 |  T4 T5 T6 |  T7 T8 T9 | T10 T11 T12|
   +-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+
         |           |           |           |
         v           v           v           v
      Task 0      Task 1      Task 2      Task 3
       core         core        core        core
   one task per partition  ->  #partitions = degree of parallelism
```

narration.text: A partition is the unit of parallelism. Spark runs one task per partition, so the number of partitions sets how much work can happen at once. Too few and you leave cores idle; too many and the scheduling overhead dominates.

slide.title: Lineage — How Spark Recovers From Failure
slide.body:
Spark does **not** replicate RDD data the way HDFS does. Instead, it remembers the *recipe* — the sequence of transformations that produced each RDD. This recipe is the **lineage graph**.

If a partition is lost (executor crashes, machine dies), Spark replays just the relevant slice of the lineage to rebuild that partition. Cheap if the lineage is short, expensive if it's long.

This is also why **caching matters**. Without `.cache()`, every action re-runs the full lineage from the source. If you reuse an RDD twice, you recompute it twice.

```text
  source --map--> rdd1 --filter--> rdd2 --reduceByKey--> rdd3
  (file)         (narrow)        (narrow)    (wide / shuffle)

  Lost a partition of rdd2?
  Spark replays just:  source -> map -> filter   for that one slice.
  No replica is ever stored — the recipe is the backup.
```

narration.text: Spark does not replicate data to survive failures. Instead each RDD records its lineage, the recipe of transformations that produced it. If an executor dies, Spark replays just the missing partition's recipe. That is the resilient in resilient distributed dataset.

slide.title: SparkContext — the Entry Point for RDDs
slide.body:
Notebook 01 used `SparkSession`. RDDs predate SparkSession and have a different entry point: `SparkContext` (`sc` by convention). SparkSession owns one — you grab it with `spark.sparkContext`.

Mental model: `spark` is the high-level door (DataFrames, SQL); `sc` is the low-level door (RDDs, broadcast variables, accumulators). Same JVM, same cluster, just two APIs.

```python
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

spark = (
    SparkSession.builder
    .appName("RDDs")
    .master("local[*]")
    .getOrCreate()
)
spark.sparkContext.setLogLevel("ERROR")
sc = spark.sparkContext

print("default parallelism:", sc.defaultParallelism)
```

narration.text: Where DataFrames start from the SparkSession, RDDs start from the SparkContext. You can reach it through spark dot sparkContext. It is the older, lower-level entry point that the session quietly wraps.

slide.title: Creating RDDs
slide.body:
An RDD comes from one of four places: **a driver collection**, **a file or external store**, **another RDD via transformation**, or **a DataFrame**. The first two and the last cover almost all explicit creation in practice:

- **`sc.parallelize(seq)`** — turn a Python list (already in the driver) into an RDD by distributing it across executors. Useful for tests and tiny demos.
- **`sc.range(start, end)`** — convenience wrapper for an RDD of longs. Same as `parallelize(range(...))` but skips materializing the Python list on the driver.
- **`sc.textFile(path)`** — read a text file (or directory of text files) into an RDD where each element is one line.
- **`sc.wholeTextFiles(path)`** — for directories of many small files; each element is a `(filename, full_content)` pair instead of one element per line.
- **`df.rdd`** — drop down from a DataFrame to its underlying RDD of `Row` objects when you need low-level control.

Every transformation in the next section (`map`, `filter`, `flatMap`, …) also produces a new RDD — in real pipelines, most RDDs come into being that way, not from an explicit creation call.

The bank capstone in `project/` reads real CSV/JSON/Parquet via the DataFrame API. We'll lean on `parallelize` here so the focus stays on RDD mechanics, not I/O.

```python
# A small set of card transactions in the bank-domain shape:
# (transaction_id, card_id, customer_id, merchant_category, amount, status)
txns = [
    ("T0001", "C001", "CUST001", "Groceries",      1200.00, "APPROVED"),
    ("T0002", "C002", "CUST002", "Travel",        18500.00, "APPROVED"),
    ("T0003", "C001", "CUST001", "Food",             450.00, "APPROVED"),
    ("T0004", "C003", "CUST003", "Shopping",        6700.00, "APPROVED"),
    ("T0005", "C002", "CUST002", "Fuel",            2800.00, "DECLINED"),
    ("T0006", "C001", "CUST001", "Entertainment",   1100.00, "APPROVED"),
    # ... 12 rows total
]

txn_rdd = sc.parallelize(txns, numSlices=4)  # explicit 4 partitions

print("partitions:", txn_rdd.getNumPartitions())
print("count     :", txn_rdd.count())
```

narration.text: There are several ways to get an RDD. Parallelize and range build one from the driver. Text file and whole text files read from storage. And df dot rdd drops a DataFrame down to its underlying rows. But in real pipelines, most RDDs are born from transforming another RDD.

slide.title: Transformations and Actions — RDD Edition
slide.body:
Same lazy model as DataFrames. Two categories:

**Transformations** build the recipe. Lazy.

| Method | Effect |
|---|---|
| `map(f)` | One in, one out. Apply `f` to each element. |
| `flatMap(f)` | One in, zero-or-more out. Flatten the result. |
| `filter(f)` | Keep elements where `f` returns truthy. |
| `distinct()` | Drop duplicates. Causes a shuffle. |
| `union(rdd2)` | Concatenate two RDDs. |
| `repartition(n)`, `coalesce(n)` | Change partition count. |

**Actions** trigger execution against the recipe.

| Method | Returns |
|---|---|
| `count()` | Number of elements |
| `take(n)` | First `n` elements |
| `first()` | First element |
| `collect()` | All elements as a Python list (driver memory) |
| `reduce(f)` | Pairwise aggregate using `f` |
| `countByValue()` | `{element: count}` dict |
| `foreach(f)` | Run `f` on each element on executors (side effects) |
| `saveAsTextFile(path)` | Write one file per partition |

```python
# Keep only APPROVED transactions, project to (merchant_category, amount).
# Two transformations — nothing has run yet.
approved = (
    txn_rdd
    .filter(lambda t: t[5] == "APPROVED")
    .map(lambda t: (t[3], t[4]))
)

print("Recipe defined. Nothing has executed yet.")
print("first 3 :", approved.take(3))   # take() is the action that triggers it
```

narration.text: Two kinds of operations. Transformations like map and filter are lazy; they just extend the recipe. Actions like collect and count are what actually run the job and hand a result back. Nothing computes until an action asks for it.

slide.title: RDDs of Objects — a Note for OOP Programmers
slide.body:
We used bare tuples above for visual brevity, but RDDs hold any type — including objects with named fields. From a JVM/Python runtime standpoint, your OOP intuition is exactly right: each element is an object, accessor methods work, GC works, closures call `.field` like in any other program.

**Python — `namedtuple` or `@dataclass`:**

```python
from collections import namedtuple
Txn = namedtuple("Txn", ["txn_id", "card_id", "cust_id",
                         "category", "amount", "status"])

txn_rdd = sc.parallelize([
    Txn("T0001", "C001", "CUST001", "Groceries", 1200.00, "APPROVED"),
    ...
])

txn_rdd.filter(lambda t: t.status == "APPROVED") \
       .map(lambda t: (t.category, t.amount))
```

**Scala — case classes are the idiomatic form:**

```scala
case class Txn(txnId: String, cardId: String, custId: String,
               category: String, amount: Double, status: String)

val txnRdd: RDD[Txn] = sc.parallelize(Seq(Txn(...), ...))
txnRdd.filter(_.status == "APPROVED").map(t => (t.category, t.amount))
```

So why does this notebook write `t[5]` instead of `t.status`? Two reasons — both pedagogical, neither principled. Tuples skip a `namedtuple` definition that would distract from the RDD mechanics being shown, and positional access makes the absence of a schema visually unmistakable. In real code, prefer named records.

**What "no schema" actually means.** Even when you write `t.status`, the JVM (or Python) accesses that property — **Spark itself does not**. To Spark's planner, every RDD element is an opaque blob it serializes between executors and hands back to your closure. Your filter lambda is a black box.

Three consequences flow from that opacity:

- **No Catalyst optimization.** Spark can't rewrite `filter(t => t.amount > 1000).map(t => t.category)` into a column-pruned, predicate-pushed-down scan, because it can't see the fields.
- **No columnar storage.** RDDs serialize whole objects across partitions; you pay I/O for fields you never read. A Parquet-backed DataFrame skips unread columns at scan time.
- **No plan-time validation.** A typo like `t.amout` blows up at runtime on an executor, not at job submission. DataFrame analysis catches `df.col("amout")` before any task runs.

Compare what Spark *sees* in each API:

```python
# RDD — Spark sees: "user-supplied function, run it on every element"
txn_rdd.filter(lambda t: t.status == "APPROVED")

# DataFrame — Spark sees: "column 'status' equals literal 'APPROVED'"
txn_df.filter(col("status") == "APPROVED")
```

The OOP mental model holds for the **runtime mechanics** (objects, properties, methods, JVM/Python execution). What it doesn't grant is **visibility into Spark's planner** — and that's the gap a DataFrame's schema fills.

narration.text: An RDD can hold any object you like, and your transformation is just an arbitrary function. That is flexible, but it also means Spark cannot see inside it. With a DataFrame, Spark understands the columns and can optimize; with an RDD it runs your code blind.

slide.title: Narrow vs Wide Transformations
slide.body:
The single most important distinction for reasoning about Spark performance.

- **Narrow transformation** — each output partition depends on **one** input partition. No data crosses the network. Examples: `map`, `filter`, `flatMap`, `mapPartitions`, `union`. These pipeline together inside a single stage.
- **Wide transformation** — each output partition depends on **many** input partitions. Data must be shuffled across the network. Examples: `reduceByKey`, `groupByKey`, `join`, `distinct`, `repartition`. Each wide transformation creates a new stage boundary.

Wide transformations are expensive — that is the rule of thumb you carry forward into the rest of the curriculum.

```text
  NARROW  (map, filter, union)        WIDE  (reduceByKey, groupByKey, join)
  one input  -> one output            many inputs -> one output  (SHUFFLE)

   P0 ---> P0'                          P0 --+    +--> P0'
   P1 ---> P1'                          P1 --+--><--+--> P1'
   P2 ---> P2'                          P2 --+    +--> P2'
   no network, same stage              data crosses network, NEW stage
```

```python
# A narrow chain — map then filter. One stage, no shuffle.
narrow = txn_rdd.map(lambda t: (t[3], t[4])).filter(lambda kv: kv[1] > 1000)
print(narrow.toDebugString().decode())

# A wide chain — reduceByKey forces a shuffle, opening a new stage.
# Look for 'ShuffledRDD' in the output — that is the shuffle boundary.
wide = txn_rdd.map(lambda t: (t[3], t[4])).reduceByKey(lambda a, b: a + b)
print(wide.toDebugString().decode())
```

narration.text: The single most important distinction. A narrow transformation, like map or filter, keeps each partition independent — no data crosses the network. A wide transformation, like groupByKey or join, must reshuffle data across partitions. Shuffles are slow, so knowing which is which tells you where the cost lives.

slide.title: Pair RDDs — the (key, value) View
slide.body:
A **Pair RDD** is just an RDD where each element is a `(key, value)` tuple. Pair RDDs unlock a family of grouping and aggregation operations that mirror SQL's `GROUP BY`.

| Method | Effect |
|---|---|
| `reduceByKey(f)` | Combine values per key using `f`. Combines locally before shuffling. |
| `groupByKey()` | Group all values per key into an iterable. Shuffles every value. |
| `mapValues(f)` | Apply `f` to values; keep keys. Narrow. |
| `flatMapValues(f)` | Apply `f`, flatten. |
| `keys()`, `values()` | Project to one side. |
| `sortByKey()` | Sort by key. Shuffles. |
| `join(rdd2)` | Inner join on key. |

To build a Pair RDD, `map` your records into `(key, value)` tuples — pick the field you want to group on as the key.

```python
# Count approved transactions per merchant category.
by_category = (
    txn_rdd
    .filter(lambda t: t[5] == "APPROVED")
    .map(lambda t: (t[3], 1))             # (merchant_category, 1)
    .reduceByKey(lambda a, b: a + b)
    .sortByKey()
)
print(by_category.collect())
```

narration.text: When an RDD holds key value pairs, a whole new family of operations opens up: the byKey methods. The key controls how Spark groups and shuffles your data, which makes pair RDDs the foundation of aggregation.

slide.title: reduceByKey vs groupByKey — the Exam Classic
slide.body:
Both can compute "sum amount per merchant category." They are not equivalent.

- **`reduceByKey`** combines values **locally on each executor first** (a map-side combine), then ships the partial results across the network. Less data crosses the wire.
- **`groupByKey`** ships **every value** for every key across the network, then aggregates on the reducing side. Much more shuffle traffic. On skewed data it can OOM the executor that owns the heavy key.

The rule: if your goal is an aggregate (sum, count, max, average) and the API has a `…ByKey` form, prefer `reduceByKey` (or `aggregateByKey` / `combineByKey`) over `groupByKey`.

```python
# Sum approved transaction amounts per merchant category.
amounts_by_cat = txn_rdd.filter(lambda t: t[5] == "APPROVED").map(lambda t: (t[3], t[4]))

# Way 1: reduceByKey — map-side combine, then shuffle
sums_reduce = amounts_by_cat.reduceByKey(lambda a, b: a + b)

# Way 2: groupByKey + mapValues(sum) — ships every value across the network
sums_group = amounts_by_cat.groupByKey().mapValues(lambda values: sum(values))

print("reduceByKey :", sorted(sums_reduce.collect()))
print("groupByKey  :", sorted(sums_group.collect()))
```

narration.text: A favourite exam question. Both can sum values per key, but reduceByKey combines partially on each partition before the shuffle, so far less data crosses the network. GroupByKey ships everything first. Reach for reduceByKey whenever your operation is associative.

slide.title: Persistence — cache(), persist(), StorageLevel
slide.body:
Default behavior: every action re-executes the entire lineage from the source. Call `.count()` and then `.collect()` on the same RDD and you computed it twice.

`.cache()` materializes the RDD on first action and reuses it after. `.persist(level)` lets you pick the storage policy.

| StorageLevel | Where the data lives |
|---|---|
| `MEMORY_ONLY` | Executor RAM. Default for `.cache()` on RDDs. Recompute on miss. |
| `MEMORY_AND_DISK` | RAM if it fits, spill to disk otherwise. Default for `.cache()` on DataFrames. |
| `DISK_ONLY` | Always to disk. Cheap memory, slow reads. |
| `*_SER` variants | Store serialized bytes. Less RAM, more CPU. |
| `*_2` variants | Replicate each partition on two executors for resilience. |

Use `.unpersist()` when you're done — caches hold executor memory.

```python
import time

def expensive_pipeline():
    return (
        sc.parallelize(range(5_000_000), numSlices=4)
        .map(lambda x: (x, x * x))
        .filter(lambda kv: kv[1] % 7 == 0)
    )

# Without cache — every action re-runs the whole pipeline.
rdd = expensive_pipeline()
rdd.count(); rdd.count()          # computed twice

# With cache — first action materializes; second hits memory.
rdd_cached = expensive_pipeline().cache()
rdd_cached.count()  # this one materializes
rdd_cached.count()  # this one hits memory
rdd_cached.unpersist()
```

narration.text: Because RDDs are lazy and lineage-based, reusing one recomputes it from scratch each time. Cache and persist break that cycle by storing the materialized partitions. Persist lets you choose the storage level — memory, disk, or a mix. Only cache data you will actually reuse.

slide.title: Partition-Level Operations
slide.body:
Three RDD methods that operate at the partition level rather than the record level.

- **`mapPartitions(f)`** — `f` receives an iterator over one partition's records and returns an iterator. Use when per-record setup is wasteful: opening a database connection, loading a model, calling an external API in batches. One setup per partition instead of per row.
- **`repartition(n)`** — full shuffle to exactly `n` partitions. Use to *increase* parallelism or rebalance skew.
- **`coalesce(n)`** — merge to `n` partitions **without** a full shuffle. Only safe when reducing partition count. Cheap, but cannot rebalance.

Rule of thumb: shrinking → `coalesce`; growing or rebalancing → `repartition`.

```python
# mapPartitions — annotate each record with how many records share its partition
def annotate(it):
    items = list(it)
    return [(len(items), x[0]) for x in items]   # (partition_size, transaction_id)

annotated = txn_rdd.mapPartitions(annotate)
print("annotated sample :", annotated.take(4))

# repartition — full shuffle to 8 partitions
print("before repartition :", txn_rdd.getNumPartitions())
print("after  repartition :", txn_rdd.repartition(8).getNumPartitions())

# coalesce — merge down to 2 partitions, no shuffle
print("after  coalesce(2) :", txn_rdd.coalesce(2).getNumPartitions())
```

narration.text: Sometimes you work a whole partition at a time. Map partitions runs once per partition — handy for setting up a connection once instead of per row. Repartition reshuffles to any number of partitions; coalesce only shrinks, and avoids a full shuffle, which makes it the cheap way to consolidate before a write.

slide.title: Shared Variables — Broadcast and Accumulators
slide.body:
Spark gives you two ways to share values across executors that are not regular RDD elements.

**Broadcast variables** ship a *read-only* value to every executor exactly once, then every task on that executor reuses it. Without broadcast, the value would be serialized into every task closure — wasteful for anything bigger than a few kilobytes. Use them for small lookup tables — a `merchant_category → spend_tier` map, an exchange-rate dict, a banned-merchant set.

**Accumulators** are *write-only-from-executors, read-only-from-driver* counters. Use them for diagnostics — counting bad records, suspicious transactions, parse failures — without breaking the lazy model. They are eventually consistent: only updates from successfully completed tasks are guaranteed.

```python
# Broadcast: a small lookup table from merchant_category to spend tier
category_tier = {
    "Groceries": "essential", "Fuel": "essential",
    "Food": "lifestyle", "Entertainment": "lifestyle",
    "Shopping": "discretionary", "Travel": "discretionary",
}
b_tier = sc.broadcast(category_tier)

# Accumulator: count high-value transactions (> 10000)
high_value_count = sc.accumulator(0)

def tag(t):
    txn_id, _, _, category, amount, _ = t
    if amount > 10000:
        high_value_count.add(1)
    return (txn_id, category, b_tier.value.get(category, "unknown"), amount)

tagged = txn_rdd.map(tag)
print(tagged.take(5))
print("high-value transactions:", high_value_count.value)
b_tier.unpersist()
```

narration.text: Two shared variables solve two problems. A broadcast variable sends one read-only copy of a lookup table to each executor, instead of bundling it with every task. An accumulator is a counter that tasks add to and the driver reads back, useful for metrics.

slide.title: RDD vs DataFrame — When to Actually Reach for Each
slide.body:
|  | RDD | DataFrame |
|---|---|---|
| API style | Functional, row-by-row | Declarative, column-based |
| Optimizer | None — your code, your plan | Catalyst rewrites the plan |
| Codegen | None — Python lambdas, JVM round-trips | Tungsten generates JVM bytecode |
| Schema | None | Required |
| Performance | Slower for structured data | Faster for structured data |
| Best for | Unstructured data, custom serialization, per-partition I/O patterns | Almost everything else |

The honest guideline for the rest of this curriculum and the bank capstone in `project/`: **prefer DataFrames**. Drop to RDDs when you need something the DataFrame API genuinely cannot express — a custom `mapPartitions` pattern, a non-tabular input, or a closure that doesn't fit Spark SQL.

narration.text: So when do you actually use raw RDDs? Almost always, prefer DataFrames — the optimizer makes them faster with less effort. Reach for RDDs only when your data has no structure, or you need precise control the higher-level API cannot give.

slide.title: One Engine, Four APIs
bullets: The vertical spine is the dataflow: Input → Read → process → Write → Output
RDD, DataFrame, SQL and pandas are interchangeable lenses over the same engine
Cross-cutting concerns (cache, shuffle, tuning) apply to every API

narration.text: Spark gives you four ways to express the same job. Pick the lens that fits the problem.

slide.title: Reading Data — spark.read
bullets: One reader for many sources: CSV, Parquet, JSON, JDBC, Delta
Control schema, format options and bad-record handling
Read multiple files / partitions as one DataFrame

slide.title: RDD API — the low-level core
bullets: create → transform → act
Narrow vs wide transformations decide where shuffles happen
Pair RDDs power key-based operations like reduceByKey

slide.title: DataFrame API — the workhorse
bullets: Typed columns with built-in functions, joins and aggregations
Catalyst optimizes the plan for you
The default choice for most batch work

slide.title: SQL API — Spark in plain SQL
bullets: Register a view, then SELECT against it
Joins, aggregations and functions in standard SQL
Same engine and optimizer as the DataFrame API

slide.title: pandas API on Spark
bullets: Write familiar pandas code, run it distributed
Bridge for analysts moving from single-node pandas
Backed by Spark's distributed execution

slide.title: Writing Data — df.write
bullets: Choose format, save mode and output layout (partitionBy)
Write to files, Hive, JDBC or Delta
Layout choices here shape how the data reads back

slide.title: Batch in a Loop
bullets: A stream is a series of micro-batches over an unbounded table
Offsets track progress; triggers pace each batch
State + checkpoint give exactly-once and fault recovery

narration.text: Structured streaming is just the batch engine, run over and over on small slices of an endless table.

slide.title: Event-time Windows
bullets: Bucket events by event time, not arrival time
Tumbling, sliding and session windows
Aggregations run per window

slide.title: Watermarking
bullets: The watermark line splits accepted from dropped late data
Bounds how long state is kept for a window
Trades a little completeness for bounded memory

slide.title: Caching & the Memory Model
bullets: cache / persist keep a reused DataFrame in memory
Storage levels trade memory for CPU and disk
Cache only what you reuse — it isn't free

slide.title: Taming the Shuffle
bullets: The shuffle is usually the bottleneck
Tune partitioning and shuffle config to match data size
Broadcast small tables to avoid shuffling the big one
